title: Chapter 7: The Vampire's Dinner Party
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D7+D4 -->
It started with an invitation.

Not to Chloe — to Sam. An old college acquaintance, Madison, who had gone to LA and transformed herself from a perfectly normal accounting major into a "brand strategist for the spiritually adjacent" (whatever that meant). Madison was hosting a dinner party in the Hollywood Hills and had, for reasons that would become clear, sent Sam an Instagram DM.
"She hasn't talked to me in three years," Sam said, staring at her phone. "She once called me 'aggressively risk-averse' to my face."
"And now she's inviting you to a party?"
"She wants numbers. Every influencer dinner party needs normies to make the influencers look more interesting by comparison."
Chloe's eyes lit up. "A gathering of vampires."
"It's a dinner party in the Hills."
"Potato, po-vampire."

The house was on Mulholland Drive. Glass walls. Infinity pool. A chandelier made of antlers that had probably cost more than Sam's annual salary. The valet looked at Shadowfax the way a sommelier looks at boxed wine.
"I'll park it myself," Sam said.
"Valet parking is complimentary," the valet said, in a tone that suggested the car was not the problem but his personal ethics were being tested.
"The Royal Steed is parked by the Royal Treasurer," Chloe announced from the passenger seat. "Protocol."
The valet looked at Sam. Sam shrugged. "She's the Queen of Avalon. I'm her CFO. The car stays with us."
The valet stepped aside. In Los Angeles, you learned not to argue with people in costumes.

They walked in. The party was exactly what the party would be: forty people who all looked like they had Instagram accounts with six-figure follower counts. A DJ playing music that sounded like a Spotify algorithm having a fever dream. A bartender making cocktails with ingredients that required explanation. And in the center of it all, Madison — blonde, wearing something that was technically a dress but spiritually a cry for attention — holding court.
"Sam!" Madison air-kissed both of Sam's cheeks without touching them. "You made it. And you brought..." She looked at Chloe. At the plastic crown. At the foam sword. At the poncho. "...a friend."
"Queen Artemis of Avalon," Chloe said. "First of her name. Liberator of Strawberry Farms. Auditor of False Prophets."

Madison's smile didn't move but something behind it did. An instinct. The instinct that said: *this person is either a performance artist or a liability, and in LA, those are the same thing until they're not.*
"How... original."
Madison was already signaling a waiter. The instinct had landed on "liability."

But before she could orchestrate a removal, a man appeared — mid-forties, tan, wearing a linen blazer with no shirt underneath. His chest was moisturized to a degree that bordered on clinical. "Who's the cosplay girl?"
"Queen Artemis," Chloe said. "And who are you?"
"Brock. I run a wellness brand. We do supplements for spiritual ascension."
"You sell vitamin pills in pretty packaging."
Brock's smile flickered. "We prefer 'bio-optimized nutrient delivery systems.'"
"That's vitamin pills with a thesaurus."
A woman slid up — yoga-toned, dressed in organic cotton, holding a drink that was green and expensive. "I love the commitment to the bit," she said. "Are you a performance artist?"
"I'm a queen," Chloe said.
"Of course. I'm Aspen. I do life coaching for people transitioning out of corporate."
"You charge people to tell them to quit their jobs?"
"I guide them through their awakening."
"That's charging people to tell them to quit their jobs."
Aspen's organic-cotton smile tightened. "It's more nuanced than that."
"Is it?"
Sam, standing three feet away with a glass of wine she had not paid for, was experiencing what could only be described as a spiritual awakening of her own.
The confrontation reached its natural peak when Brock made the mistake of asking: "So what do you do?"
"I liberate souls from the engines of late-stage capitalism."
"I'm sorry?"

"Yesterday I liberated a strawberry farm from vanity demons. Before that, a wellness guru named Kevin. Before that, a gas station troll and a convenience store goblin. You're all the same thing, really — people who've forgotten that the only thing anyone actually needs is other people, and everything else is just a timeshare on happiness."
The room had gone quiet. The DJ had stopped his algorithmic fever dream. Forty influencers with six-figure follower counts were staring at a woman in a plastic crown.
"That's..." Brock started, then stopped. Because he couldn't figure out if it was an insult or the most honest thing anyone had said to him in years.
Madison appeared at Sam's elbow. "Your friend is ruining my party."
Sam took a sip of wine. "She's improving it."
"People are uncomfortable."
"Good. Uncomfortable is the first step toward something other than hollow."
Madison stared at her. "You've changed."
"I've been on a road trip with a woman who thinks she's the Queen of Avalon. It does something to your perspective."
Across the room, Aspen had put down her green drink. She was looking at Chloe with an expression that was confusing to everyone, including herself. It might have been respect.
"How much for a session?" Aspen asked.
"Free," Chloe said. "Grass is free. Self-reflection is free."
"But how do you monetize that?"
"I don't."
Aspen looked like someone had just shown her a color she'd never seen before.

Two hours later, Chloe had a small crowd around her on the infinity pool deck. Not because she'd charmed them — she hadn't — but because she was the most honest thing in a room built on curated dishonesty, and that was more intoxicating than any green cocktail.
"And that's when I told the gas station troll that the curse could still be lifted," Chloe was saying. "Grass is free."
"What does that mean?" someone asked.
"It means go outside. Touch something real. Call someone who knew you before you had followers."
A man who ran a crypto startup looked at his phone, then put it in his pocket. A woman who did "aura photography" for celebrities looked at her hands like she was seeing them for the first time.
Sam found Madison near the bar, staring at her own party like a general watching a battle she was losing.
"They're listening to her," Madison said. "They never listen to anyone."
"She's not selling anything."
"That's the worst part. You can't compete with free."

They left at midnight. Shadowfax's engine sounded worse than ever — a grinding noise that would need to be addressed, possibly with prayer — but it ran. Chloe leaned her head against the window, crown tilted, exhausted and triumphant.
"The vampires have been subdued," she murmured.
"You made a crypto bro put his phone in his pocket. That's a war crime."
"It's liberation."
Sam drove in silence for a moment. Then: "Madison said I've changed."
"Have you?"
"I don't know yet."
Chloe smiled, eyes still closed. "That's how you know you're changing. When you don't know yet."

They drove through the LA night — past billboards for movies that wouldn't be good, past palm trees that looked like they were trying too hard, past a city of people performing their lives for audiences of strangers. Two women in a dying Honda Civic, one with a plastic crown, one with a spreadsheet, driving toward whatever came next.
